sub_topics.title: Ukusalimusha (Greeting People)
competences: Learners should be able to greet people in Icibemba
Learners should be able to respond to greetings appropriately
Learners should be able to use correct greetings for different times of day

instructional_blocks.hook: Teacher greets learners in Icibemba and encourages them to respond
teacher_steps: Greet learners using morning, afternoon and evening greetings in Icibemba
Model correct responses to greetings
Use pictures/flashcards showing people greeting each other
Demonstrate greetings with gestures
Guide learners to practice greetings in pairs
learner_steps: Listen to the teacher's greetings
Respond to greetings as modeled by the teacher
Practice greetings with a partner
Role-play greeting scenarios
Sing greeting songs in Icibemba

instructional_blocks.hook: Show pictures of people meeting and greeting in different situations
teacher_steps: Show pictures of people greeting in different contexts
Ask learners to identify the type of greeting being used
Introduce key greeting vocabulary
Model greetings for elders, peers and younger people
Facilitate a greeting circle activity
learner_steps: Observe pictures and identify greetings
Repeat greeting phrases after the teacher
Practice greeting elders respectfully
Participate in the greeting circle
Draw a picture of people greeting

sub_topics.short_notes: Amatendo (Greetings) are an important part of Icibemba culture and communication. In Icibemba, greetings vary according to the time of day and the relationship between the people involved. Morning greetings include 'Mwashibukeni' (Good morning - said to one person) and the response is 'Nashibuka' or 'Twashibuka'. Afternoon greetings use 'Mwabombeni' and evening greetings use 'Mwabukeni'. When greeting elders, children are expected to show respect through both words and body language, such as slightly kneeling or clapping hands. Peer greetings may be more informal. Greetings establish social bonds and are a sign of respect in Bemba culture. It is important for ECE learners to learn and practice these greetings as part of their cultural identity and language development.

vocabulary.definition: Greetings - the act of acknowledging and welcoming another person
Good morning - a greeting used in the morning hours
I have woken up well - a response to the morning greeting
Good afternoon - a greeting used during the afternoon
Good evening - a greeting used in the evening
To greet someone - the action of giving a greeting

questions: Can the learner greet using the correct Icibemba greeting for the time of day?
Can the learner respond correctly when greeted?
Does the learner show respect when greeting elders?
Can the learner use at least three different greetings in Icibemba?

sub_topics.title: Ukweba Ishina (Telling Your Name)
competences: Learners should be able to state their own name in Icibemba
Learners should be able to ask others their names
Learners should be able to introduce themselves and others

instructional_blocks.hook: Teacher introduces himself/herself by name and asks learners who they are
teacher_steps: Introduce yourself using 'Ishina lyandi ndi...' (My name is...)
Ask individual learners 'Ishina lyobe ndi nani?' (What is your name?)
Model the correct response structure
Use name cards with each learner's name written
Play a name game where learners say their names around the circle
learner_steps: Listen as the teacher introduces himself/herself
Respond with their own name when asked
Practice asking a partner their name
Hold up their name card when their name is called
Introduce a friend to the class

instructional_blocks.hook: Use a puppet or doll and ask learners what the puppet's name should be
teacher_steps: Introduce a puppet/doll and give it a Bemba name
Model introduction phrases using the puppet
Teach the phrase 'Uyu ndi...' (This is...)
Facilitate pair introductions
Create a class name chart with all learners' names
learner_steps: Suggest a name for the puppet
Practice introducing the puppet to classmates
Introduce their partner to the class in Icibemba
Find their name on the class name chart
Draw and label a picture of themselves

sub_topics.short_notes: In Icibemba culture, names (Amaina) carry great significance. Children are often given names that reflect circumstances of birth, family history, ancestors, or hopes for the child's future. The phrase used to state one's name is 'Ishina lyandi ndi...' meaning 'My name is...'. To ask someone's name, one says 'Ishina lyobe ndi nani?' meaning 'What is your name?' When introducing another person, 'Uyu ndi...' (This is...) is used. At the ECE level, learners are developing their identity and sense of self. Learning to say and recognize their name in Icibemba is a foundational literacy and language skill. It also connects learners to their cultural heritage and helps build confidence in speaking the language.

vocabulary.definition: Names - the words used to identify a person
Name - the word used to identify a specific person
My name - used when stating one's own name
Your name - used when referring to another person's name
Who is - used in asking for a person's identity or name
This (person) - used when pointing to or introducing someone nearby

questions: Can the learner state their own name in Icibemba?
Can the learner ask a classmate their name using the correct Icibemba phrase?
Can the learner introduce another person in Icibemba?
Can the learner recognize their written name?

sub_topics.title: Ukweba Imibele ya Muntu (Naming Body Parts)
competences: Learners should be able to identify and name body parts in Icibemba
Learners should be able to point to body parts when named
Learners should be able to use body part vocabulary in simple sentences

instructional_blocks.hook: Teacher touches different body parts and asks learners to name them
teacher_steps: Point to and name body parts in Icibemba (head - mutwe, eyes - meso, nose - mpumbu, mouth - akanwa, ears - matwi, hands - maasa, legs - malundi)
Use a large body chart or doll to point to body parts
Sing a body parts song in Icibemba
Play 'Simon Says' using Icibemba body part names
Ask learners to point to body parts on themselves
learner_steps: Listen and repeat the names of body parts
Point to body parts when named by the teacher
Sing the body parts song
Participate in the 'Simon Says' game
Label body parts on a drawn outline of a person

instructional_blocks.hook: Play a body parts song and have learners move the body part being named
teacher_steps: Review body parts from previous lesson
Introduce function of each body part (eyes for seeing, ears for hearing, etc.)
Use sentence frames: 'Ndemona na meso yandi' (I see with my eyes)
Create a body parts matching activity with pictures and words
Guide learners to draw and label body parts
learner_steps: Name body parts when pointed to on the chart
State the function of a body part in Icibemba
Complete the matching activity
Draw a person and label at least 4 body parts
Play a body parts guessing game with a partner

sub_topics.short_notes: Imibele (body parts) is an essential vocabulary topic for ECE learners. Knowing the names of body parts in Icibemba helps learners describe themselves, understand health-related instructions, and communicate about their physical experiences. Key body parts in Icibemba include: mutwe (head), meso (eyes), mpumbu (nose), akanwa (mouth), matwi (ears), maasa (hands), malundi (legs), chibondo (stomach), and umutima (heart). Learning body part vocabulary also supports the development of body awareness, which is important for the holistic development of young children. Teachers should use physical, hands-on activities and songs to make learning body parts engaging and memorable for ECE learners.

vocabulary.definition: Body parts - the different parts that make up the human body
Head - the upper part of the body containing the brain and face
Eyes - the organs used for seeing
Nose - the organ used for smelling and breathing
Mouth - the opening used for eating, drinking and speaking
Ears - the organs used for hearing
Hands - the body parts at the end of the arms used for holding and touching
Legs - the body parts used for walking and running
Stomach - the body part used for digesting food

questions: Can the learner correctly name at least 5 body parts in Icibemba?
Can the learner point to a body part when the teacher names it in Icibemba?
Can the learner state one function of a named body part?
Can the learner identify body parts on a picture or drawing?

sub_topics.title: Inombwa (Animals)
competences: Learners should be able to name common animals in Icibemba
Learners should be able to identify animals by their sounds
Learners should be able to categorize animals as domestic or wild

instructional_blocks.hook: Make animal sounds and ask learners to identify which animal makes that sound
teacher_steps: Show pictures or models of common animals
Name each animal in Icibemba (imbwa - dog, nkoko - chicken, ng'ombe - cow, fisi - hyena, nkalamu - lion, nsofu - elephant)
Make animal sounds and have learners identify the animal
Sort animals into domestic and wild categories
Tell a short story featuring animals in Icibemba
learner_steps: Listen and repeat animal names in Icibemba
Match animal pictures to their Icibemba names
Make animal sounds when the animal is shown
Sort animal pictures into domestic and wild groups
Listen to and retell the animal story

sub_topics.short_notes: Inombwa (animals) are a familiar and engaging topic for young learners. In Icibemba, common domestic animals include: imbwa (dog), nkoko (chicken), ng'ombe (cow), mbushi (goat), and nkama (cat). Common wild animals include: nkalamu (lion), nsofu (elephant), fisi (hyena), ingwe (leopard), and imfwiti (wolf). Learning animal names in Icibemba connects learners to their natural environment and local culture. Animals feature prominently in Bemba folk tales and proverbs, making this vocabulary particularly culturally relevant. ECE learners can use animal names in songs, stories, and games to reinforce vocabulary.

vocabulary.definition: Animals - living creatures that are not plants or human beings
Dog - a common domestic animal kept as a pet or for security
Chicken - a common domestic bird kept for eggs and meat
Cow - a large domestic animal kept for milk and meat
Lion - a large wild cat, king of the jungle
Elephant - the largest land animal, found in the wild
Hyena - a wild scavenging animal known for its laugh-like call

questions: Can the learner name at least 4 animals in Icibemba?
Can the learner identify the sound of a named animal?
Can the learner sort animals into domestic and wild categories?
Can the learner use animal names in a simple sentence?

sub_topics.title: Ifisuba (Colours)
competences: Learners should be able to name basic colours in Icibemba
Learners should be able to identify colours in the classroom environment
Learners should be able to describe objects using colour words in Icibemba

instructional_blocks.hook: Hold up coloured objects and ask learners if they know the colour names in Icibemba
teacher_steps: Introduce colour names using coloured cards or objects (fikungu - red, buluu - blue, fyelu - yellow, citete - green, fwii - black, yelu - white)
Show objects of each colour and repeat the colour name
Play a colour sorting game
Teach a colours song in Icibemba
Create a colour chart for the classroom wall
learner_steps: Repeat colour names after the teacher
Point to objects of a named colour in the classroom
Sort coloured objects into colour groups
Sing the colours song
Colour a picture using the correct colours as instructed in Icibemba

sub_topics.short_notes: Ifisuba (colours) vocabulary is an important part of early language development. In Icibemba, the basic colour terms include: fikungu (red), buluu (blue), fyelu (yellow), citete (green), fwii (black), and yelu (white). Some colours in Icibemba are loanwords from English due to historical contact. Knowing colour names allows learners to describe the world around them, follow instructions, and engage in creative activities. Colour vocabulary also supports mathematical concepts such as sorting and classification. Teachers should use real objects, coloured materials, and artwork to make colour learning concrete and meaningful for ECE learners.

vocabulary.definition: Colours - the visual properties of objects determined by light
Red - the colour of blood and fire
Blue - the colour of the sky and water
Yellow - the colour of the sun and bananas
Green - the colour of grass and leaves
Black - the darkest colour, absence of light
White - the lightest colour, colour of clouds and milk

questions: Can the learner correctly name at least 4 colours in Icibemba?
Can the learner point to an object of a named colour?
Can the learner use a colour word to describe an object (e.g. 'ichitabo cha buluu' - blue book)?
Can the learner sort objects by colour?

sub_topics.title: Abantu Abakabomba (People Who Work)
competences: Learners should be able to name common occupations in Icibemba
Learners should be able to describe what different workers do
Learners should be able to express what they want to be when they grow up

instructional_blocks.hook: Ask learners what their parents do for work and how to say it in Icibemba
teacher_steps: Show pictures of people in different occupations
Introduce occupation vocabulary in Icibemba (umupolisi - police officer, umusumba - nurse, umudokota - doctor, umubilikimo - teacher, umusenshi - soldier, umuimi - farmer)
Describe what each worker does using simple Icibemba sentences
Role-play different occupations
Ask learners what they want to be when they grow up
learner_steps: Identify workers in pictures
Repeat occupation names in Icibemba
Role-play their chosen occupation
Say their desired occupation: 'Nkakala umubilikimo' (I will be a teacher)
Draw a picture of themselves in their chosen occupation

sub_topics.short_notes: Understanding different occupations (Inshila ya Kubomba) helps ECE learners appreciate the roles different people play in society. Common occupations in Icibemba include: umubilikimo (teacher), umusumba (nurse), umudokota (doctor), umupolisi (police officer), umuimi (farmer), umusenshi (soldier), umupuushi (driver), and umutongi (builder). Discussing occupations helps learners develop vocabulary, build career awareness from an early age, and understand the community they live in. It also provides an opportunity to discuss gender equality by showing that both men and women can do any job. The sentence structure 'Nkakala...' (I will be...) can be used to help learners express their aspirations.

vocabulary.definition: Occupation/Work - the job or type of work a person does
Teacher - a person who teaches and educates others
Nurse - a healthcare worker who cares for sick patients
Doctor - a medical professional who diagnoses and treats illnesses
Police officer - a person who enforces laws and maintains order
Farmer - a person who grows crops or raises animals for food
Soldier - a person who serves in the military to protect the country

questions: Can the learner name at least 3 occupations in Icibemba?
Can the learner describe what a named worker does?
Can the learner say what they want to be when they grow up in Icibemba?
Can the learner match a worker to the tool they use (e.g. doctor - stethoscope)?

sub_topics.title: Inshimi (Folk Tales)
competences: Learners should be able to listen attentively to stories told in Icibemba
Learners should be able to retell key events from a story
Learners should be able to identify the moral lesson of a story

instructional_blocks.hook: Begin with the traditional story-opening phrase 'Pali cine cine...' and have learners respond 'Cine cine!'
teacher_steps: Use the traditional Bemba story opening 'Pali cine cine...' to signal story time
Tell a traditional Bemba folk tale using expressive voice, gestures and props
Pause during the story to check comprehension
Ask questions about characters, events and morals
Encourage learners to retell parts of the story
learner_steps: Respond to the story opening phrase
Listen attentively to the folk tale
Answer comprehension questions about the story
Act out parts of the story
Retell the story to a partner or the class

instructional_blocks.hook: Show pictures from a story and ask learners to predict what will happen
teacher_steps: Use picture books or hand-drawn pictures to support storytelling
Teach story sequence using beginning, middle and end
Model story retelling using picture prompts
Facilitate group storytelling where each learner adds a sentence
Create a class story book
learner_steps: Sequence story pictures in the correct order
Retell the story using pictures as prompts
Add their part to the group story
Draw their favourite part of the story
Share the moral lesson of the story

sub_topics.short_notes: Ukulundula (storytelling) is a central part of Bemba oral tradition and culture. Traditional folk tales (Inshimi) were historically told in the evenings around the fire and served to pass on cultural values, moral lessons, and historical knowledge from generation to generation. The traditional opening 'Pali cine cine...' signals the beginning of a story and learners respond 'Cine cine!' to show they are ready to listen. Common themes in Bemba folk tales include respect for elders, the importance of hard work, consequences of greed or dishonesty, and the cleverness of small animals overcoming large ones. At ECE level, storytelling develops listening comprehension, vocabulary, imagination, and early literacy skills. Teachers should use expressive storytelling techniques including varied voice, gestures, and visual aids to bring stories to life.

vocabulary.definition: Storytelling - the art of telling or narrating a story
Folk tales - traditional stories passed down through generations
Once upon a time - the traditional opening phrase for a Bemba story
Message/Moral - the lesson or meaning conveyed by a story
Characters - the people or animals that appear in a story

questions: Can the learner listen attentively during story time?
Can the learner answer at least 2 questions about a story heard?
Can the learner retell the main events of a story using pictures?
Can the learner identify the moral lesson of a folk tale?

sub_topics.title: Ifya Kulya (Types of Food)
competences: Learners should be able to name common foods in Icibemba
Learners should be able to talk about foods they like and dislike
Learners should be able to identify healthy and unhealthy foods

instructional_blocks.hook: Bring in real food items or pictures of food and ask learners to name them in Icibemba
teacher_steps: Display pictures or models of common Zambian foods
Introduce food names in Icibemba (ubwali - nshima, ifisashi - relish, inama - meat, ifyumbu - sweet potatoes, impwa - tomatoes, bondwe - vegetables)
Teach the phrases 'Natemwa...' (I like...) and 'Naukisha...' (I do not like...)
Sort foods into healthy and less healthy categories
Teach food and nutrition vocabulary through a cooking song
learner_steps: Name foods shown in pictures in Icibemba
Say which foods they like and dislike using the correct phrases
Sort food pictures into healthy and less healthy groups
Sing the food song
Draw their favourite meal and label the foods in Icibemba

sub_topics.short_notes: Food (Ifya Kulya) vocabulary is relevant and meaningful for ECE learners as food is part of their daily lives. Key food items in Icibemba include: ubwali (nshima - the staple food made from maize flour), ifisashi (relish - cooked vegetables or legumes eaten with nshima), inama (meat), amafi (milk), ifyumbu (sweet potatoes), impwa (tomatoes), icibwabwa (pumpkin), bondwe (leafy greens), and ubowa (mushrooms). Learning to talk about food in Icibemba supports vocabulary development, cultural connection, and health education. The concept of balanced meals and nutrition can be introduced at this level using simple language. Foods that are locally available and culturally familiar should be prioritized in teaching to make learning relevant and contextual.

vocabulary.definition: Food - substances eaten to provide nutrition and energy
Nshima - the staple food of Zambia, made from maize flour
Relish - cooked vegetables, legumes or meat eaten alongside nshima
Meat - animal flesh used as food
Milk - a nutritious liquid food produced by mammals
Sweet potatoes - a sweet root vegetable grown in Zambia
Leafy vegetables/greens - leaves of plants used as food

questions: Can the learner name at least 4 foods in Icibemba?
Can the learner say which foods they like and dislike using correct Icibemba phrases?
Can the learner sort foods into healthy and less healthy categories?
Can the learner name the main Zambian staple food in Icibemba?
